statement: Remote work has grown significantly in Iceland since 2020, with approximately 25–30% of the workforce working remotely at least part-time in 2025. Iceland's high-speed internet penetration (~98% of households) and strong digital infrastructure make it attractive for remote workers. The EU's proposed Digital Nomad framework and ongoing digital single market integration could benefit Iceland — currently, EEA membership does not provide equivalent access to all EU digital market initiatives. EU membership would bring Iceland into the full scope of the Digital Services Act and Digital Markets Act, and could simplify cross-border remote work taxation and social security coordination. Iceland already offers a long-term visa for non-EEA remote workers.
source_name: Hagstofa Íslands — ICT usage survey; European Commission — Digital Economy and Society Index (DESI)
caveats: Remote work statistics vary by definition (fully remote vs hybrid). The digital single market's relevance to Iceland is limited by market size — most digital services operate globally regardless of EU membership. Cross-border tax coordination for remote work remains unresolved even within the EU.

related_entries.statement: Iceland already participates in the free movement of workers through the EEA Agreement, which has been in force since 1994. EEA nationals have the right to live and work in Iceland without work permits, and Icelanders have reciprocal rights across the EU/EEA. EU membership would not create new free movement rights beyond what already exists under the EEA framework. However, EU membership would give Iceland voting rights in shaping the rules governing free movement, whereas under the EEA Iceland implements EU single market legislation without a formal vote in the Council or European Parliament.

Since the EEA Agreement entered into force in 1994, Iceland has incorporated approximately 13,000 EU legal acts into Icelandic law through the EEA Joint Committee. As of 2025, the EEA acquis covers roughly 75% of the EU single market legislation, excluding agriculture, fisheries, customs union, trade policy, justice and home affairs, foreign policy, and the euro. Iceland has a formal right to veto EEA-relevant legislation through the 'right of reservation' under Article 102 EEA, but this right has never been exercised due to the severe consequences — it would trigger suspension of the affected part of the EEA Agreement.
